name: Translational motion, forces, work, energy, and equilibrium in living systems

nodes.label: Units, dimensions, and vectors
nodes.summary: Physics of motion is built on consistent SI units, dimensional analysis, and vector/scalar quantities.

nodes.label: Units and dimensional analysis
nodes.summary: Physical quantities are expressed in SI base and derived units and checked by dimensional analysis.

nodes.label: SI base units
nodes.summary: The mechanical SI base units are the meter (length), kilogram (mass), and second (time).

nodes.label: Derived units
nodes.summary: Derived units combine base units, e.g. newton (kg·m/s²), joule (kg·m²/s²), and watt (J/s).

nodes.label: Dimensional analysis
nodes.summary: Equations must be dimensionally consistent, and units can be tracked to convert and validate results.

nodes.label: Metric prefixes
nodes.summary: Prefixes such as kilo (10³), centi (10⁻²), milli (10⁻³), micro (10⁻⁶), and nano (10⁻⁹) scale SI units.

nodes.label: Unit conversion (factor-label method)
nodes.summary: Quantities are converted between units by multiplying by ratios equal to one (e.g. km/h to m/s).

nodes.label: Scalars vs vectors
nodes.summary: Quantities are scalars (magnitude only) or vectors (magnitude and direction).

nodes.label: Scalar quantities
nodes.summary: Scalars such as distance, speed, mass, time, and energy have magnitude but no direction.

nodes.label: Vector quantities
nodes.summary: Vectors such as displacement, velocity, acceleration, force, and momentum have both magnitude and direction.

nodes.label: Vector addition (tip-to-tail / parallelogram)
nodes.summary: Vectors add tip-to-tail or by the parallelogram rule, and the resultant is their geometric sum.

nodes.label: Vector subtraction
nodes.summary: Subtracting a vector means adding its negative (reversed direction).

nodes.label: Vector components (Ax = A cosθ, Ay = A sinθ)
nodes.summary: A vector resolves into perpendicular components Ax = A cosθ and Ay = A sinθ relative to an axis.

nodes.label: Magnitude and direction from components
nodes.summary: Magnitude is A = √(Ax² + Ay²) and direction is θ = arctan(Ay/Ax).

nodes.label: Scalar multiplication of a vector
nodes.summary: Multiplying a vector by a scalar scales its magnitude and, for a negative scalar, reverses its direction.

nodes.label: Dot (scalar) product
nodes.summary: The dot product A·B = AB cosθ yields a scalar and underlies the definition of work.

nodes.label: Cross (vector) product
nodes.summary: The cross product A×B = AB sinθ yields a vector perpendicular to both and underlies torque.

nodes.label: Right-hand rule for cross products
nodes.summary: The right-hand rule gives the direction of a cross product (and thus torque or angular vectors).

nodes.label: Unit vectors (î, ĵ, k̂)
nodes.summary: Unit vectors î, ĵ, k̂ have magnitude one and define the x, y, z directions for component notation.

nodes.label: Kinematics (translational motion)
nodes.summary: Kinematics describes translational motion through displacement, velocity, and acceleration without reference to its causes.

nodes.label: Position, displacement, velocity, acceleration
nodes.summary: Motion is characterized by position and its first and second time derivatives.

nodes.label: Distance vs displacement
nodes.summary: Distance is the total path length (scalar) while displacement is the straight-line change in position (vector).

nodes.label: Speed vs velocity
nodes.summary: Speed is distance/time (scalar) while velocity is displacement/time (vector).

nodes.label: Average vs instantaneous velocity
nodes.summary: Average velocity is Δx/Δt over an interval while instantaneous velocity is the limit as Δt→0 (slope of x-t graph).

nodes.label: Acceleration
nodes.summary: Acceleration a = Δv/Δt is the rate of change of velocity, a vector with units m/s².

nodes.label: Average vs instantaneous acceleration
nodes.summary: Average acceleration is Δv/Δt over an interval while instantaneous acceleration is its limit as Δt→0.

nodes.label: Acceleration vs deceleration (sign conventions)
nodes.summary: Speed increases when a and v point the same way and decreases when they oppose, regardless of sign.

nodes.label: Reference frames and relative velocity
nodes.summary: Velocity is measured relative to a reference frame and transforms by vector addition between frames.

nodes.label: Inertial reference frames
nodes.summary: Motion is described relative to a frame; an inertial frame is non-accelerating, where Newton's laws hold.

nodes.label: Relative velocity (vector addition of velocities)
nodes.summary: The velocity of A relative to C equals v(A/B) + v(B/C), e.g. swimmer-in-current or boat-crossing problems.

nodes.label: Uniformly accelerated motion (kinematic equations)
nodes.summary: Constant-acceleration motion is solved with the four kinematic (SUVAT) equations.

nodes.label: v = v₀ + at
nodes.summary: Final velocity equals initial velocity plus acceleration times time for constant acceleration.

nodes.label: x = v₀t + ½at²
nodes.summary: Displacement equals initial-velocity time term plus one-half acceleration times time squared.

nodes.label: v² = v₀² + 2aΔx
nodes.summary: Final velocity squared equals initial velocity squared plus 2aΔx, eliminating time.

nodes.label: x = ½(v₀ + v)t
nodes.summary: Displacement equals the average of initial and final velocity times time.

nodes.label: Validity (constant acceleration only)
nodes.summary: The kinematic equations apply only when acceleration is constant over the interval analyzed.

nodes.label: Free fall and projectile motion
nodes.summary: Objects under gravity alone undergo free fall, and launched objects follow parabolic projectile paths.

nodes.label: Acceleration due to gravity (g ≈ 9.8 m/s²)
nodes.summary: Near Earth's surface all objects fall with acceleration g ≈ 9.8 m/s² downward, independent of mass.

nodes.label: Free fall (motion under gravity only)
nodes.summary: Free fall is motion influenced only by gravity, with constant downward acceleration g and zero horizontal acceleration.

nodes.label: Effect of air resistance on falling bodies
nodes.summary: Air resistance opposes motion and, when present, makes heavier or denser objects fall faster than light ones.

nodes.label: Terminal velocity
nodes.summary: A falling body reaches terminal (constant) velocity when drag balances weight so net force and acceleration are zero.

nodes.label: Independence of horizontal and vertical motion
nodes.summary: In projectile motion horizontal velocity is constant while vertical motion is governed independently by gravity.

nodes.label: Horizontal projectile component (constant vx)
nodes.summary: Horizontal velocity vx = v₀cosθ stays constant because no horizontal force acts (ignoring air resistance).

nodes.label: Vertical projectile component (vy with g)
nodes.summary: Vertical velocity vy = v₀sinθ − gt and the object follows free-fall kinematics in the vertical direction.

nodes.label: Time of flight, max height, and range
nodes.summary: Flight time, peak height, and horizontal range are found by combining the independent vertical and horizontal equations.

nodes.label: Launch angle for maximum range (45°)
nodes.summary: On level ground (no air resistance) horizontal range is maximized at a 45° launch angle, and complementary angles share a range.

nodes.label: Trajectory symmetry and apex
nodes.summary: At the apex vy = 0, and a level-ground trajectory is symmetric with equal ascent and descent times.

nodes.label: Parabolic trajectory shape
nodes.summary: Combining constant horizontal velocity with uniform vertical acceleration gives a parabolic path.

nodes.label: Motion graphs
nodes.summary: Position-, velocity-, and acceleration-time graphs encode motion through slopes and areas.

nodes.label: Position–time graph (slope = velocity)
nodes.summary: The slope of a position–time graph gives instantaneous velocity.

nodes.label: Velocity–time graph (slope = a, area = Δx)
nodes.summary: On a velocity–time graph the slope is acceleration and the area under the curve is displacement.

nodes.label: Acceleration–time graph (area = Δv)
nodes.summary: The area under an acceleration–time graph gives the change in velocity.

nodes.label: Slope vs area interpretation of motion graphs
nodes.summary: Slopes give rates (derivatives) and areas give accumulations (integrals), linking the three motion graphs.

nodes.label: Forces and Newton's laws (dynamics)
nodes.summary: Forces are pushes/pulls that change motion according to Newton's three laws of motion.

nodes.label: Newton's three laws
nodes.summary: Newton's laws relate net force to inertia, acceleration, and interaction pairs.

nodes.label: First law (inertia)
nodes.summary: An object at rest stays at rest and one in motion stays in uniform motion unless acted on by a net external force.

nodes.label: Inertia and mass
nodes.summary: Inertia is an object's resistance to changes in motion and is quantified by its mass.

nodes.label: Mass vs weight
nodes.summary: Mass is an intrinsic scalar measure of inertia while weight is the gravitational force on that mass and varies with g.

nodes.label: Second law (F = ma)
nodes.summary: Net force equals mass times acceleration (ΣF = ma), with acceleration in the direction of net force.

nodes.label: Net force and force balance
nodes.summary: The net (resultant) force is the vector sum of all forces and determines acceleration.

nodes.label: Third law (action–reaction)
nodes.summary: For every action force there is an equal and opposite reaction force on the other body.

nodes.label: Identifying action–reaction pairs
nodes.summary: Action–reaction pairs act on different objects and therefore never cancel on a single body.

nodes.label: Free-body diagrams
nodes.summary: A free-body diagram isolates one object and shows all external forces as vectors for ΣF analysis.

nodes.label: Types of forces
nodes.summary: Mechanics problems involve gravity, normal, tension, friction, applied, and spring forces.

nodes.label: Weight (W = mg)
nodes.summary: Weight is the gravitational force on a mass, W = mg, directed toward Earth's center.

nodes.label: Normal force
nodes.summary: The normal force is the contact force perpendicular to a surface that prevents interpenetration.

nodes.label: Tension
nodes.summary: Tension is the pulling force transmitted along a rope, cable, or muscle/tendon.

nodes.label: Applied / contact push force
nodes.summary: An applied force is any external push or pull exerted directly on an object.

nodes.label: Spring (restoring) force
nodes.summary: A spring exerts a restoring force opposing its displacement (treated via Hooke's law).

nodes.label: Hooke's law (F = −kx)
nodes.summary: A spring's restoring force is proportional to and opposite the displacement, F = −kx, where k is the spring constant.

nodes.label: Drag / air resistance force
nodes.summary: Drag is a velocity-dependent resistive force from a fluid that opposes an object's motion through it.

nodes.label: Contact vs field (action-at-a-distance) forces
nodes.summary: Contact forces require touching surfaces while field forces (gravity, electromagnetic) act at a distance.

nodes.label: Fundamental forces of nature
nodes.summary: The four fundamental forces are gravitational, electromagnetic, strong nuclear, and weak nuclear.

nodes.label: Newtonian gravitation
nodes.summary: Masses attract via the universal law of gravitation, which defines g near Earth.

nodes.label: Law of universal gravitation (F = Gm₁m₂/r²)
nodes.summary: Every two masses attract with force F = Gm₁m₂/r², an inverse-square law along the line joining them.

nodes.label: Gravitational constant G
nodes.summary: G ≈ 6.67×10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg² is the universal gravitational constant.

nodes.label: Surface gravity g = GM/R²
nodes.summary: The local gravitational acceleration equals GM/R², linking weight to a planet's mass and radius.

nodes.label: Inverse-square distance dependence
nodes.summary: Gravitational force falls off as 1/r², so doubling separation quarters the force.

nodes.label: Variation of g with altitude and location
nodes.summary: Local g decreases with altitude (greater r) and varies slightly with latitude and planetary body.

nodes.label: Center of mass
nodes.summary: The center of mass is the mass-weighted average position where gravity effectively acts and translational motion is tracked.

nodes.label: Center of gravity
nodes.summary: The center of gravity is the point where total weight acts, coinciding with center of mass in uniform gravity.

nodes.label: Apparent weight and accelerating frames
nodes.summary: In an accelerating frame the normal force (apparent weight) differs from true weight.

nodes.label: Apparent weight in an elevator
nodes.summary: In an accelerating elevator apparent weight N = m(g ± a), exceeding mg when accelerating up and less when down.

nodes.label: Weightlessness (apparent) in free fall
nodes.summary: In free fall the normal force is zero, producing apparent weightlessness even though gravity still acts.

nodes.label: Friction
nodes.summary: Friction is a contact force opposing relative motion or its tendency between surfaces.

nodes.label: Static and kinetic friction
nodes.summary: Friction is static before sliding and kinetic during sliding, each with its own coefficient.

nodes.label: Static friction (fs ≤ μsN)
nodes.summary: Static friction adjusts up to a maximum fs,max = μsN to prevent the start of sliding.

nodes.label: Kinetic friction (fk = μkN)
nodes.summary: Kinetic friction fk = μkN opposes motion at constant magnitude while surfaces slide.

nodes.label: μs > μk relationship
nodes.summary: The coefficient of static friction is generally greater than the kinetic coefficient for the same surfaces.

nodes.label: Coefficient of friction (μ)
nodes.summary: The dimensionless coefficient μ depends on the two surfaces and relates friction to normal force.

nodes.label: Rolling friction
nodes.summary: Rolling friction resists a rolling object and is generally much smaller than sliding kinetic friction.

nodes.label: Properties of friction
nodes.summary: Friction depends on normal force and surfaces but not on apparent contact area or speed.

nodes.label: Dependence on normal force
nodes.summary: Friction is proportional to the normal force pressing the surfaces together.

nodes.label: Independence from contact area
nodes.summary: To the MCAT model, friction does not depend on the apparent surface contact area.

nodes.label: Direction opposes relative motion
nodes.summary: Friction always acts parallel to the surface, opposing the direction of (impending) relative sliding.

nodes.label: Angle of repose (tanθ = μs)
nodes.summary: An object on an incline begins to slide when tanθ exceeds μs, so the critical angle satisfies tanθ = μs.

nodes.label: Uniform circular motion and centripetal force
nodes.summary: Objects moving in circles at constant speed require a centripetal force directed toward the center.

nodes.label: Centripetal acceleration and force
nodes.summary: Circular motion involves inward (centripetal) acceleration produced by a net inward force.

nodes.label: Centripetal acceleration (a = v²/r)
nodes.summary: Centripetal acceleration ac = v²/r points toward the circle's center even at constant speed.

nodes.label: Centripetal force (F = mv²/r)
nodes.summary: The net inward (centripetal) force Fc = mv²/r is required to keep an object in circular motion.

nodes.label: Velocity tangent, acceleration radial
nodes.summary: In uniform circular motion velocity is tangent to the path while acceleration points radially inward.

nodes.label: Sources of centripetal force
nodes.summary: Centripetal force can be supplied by tension, gravity, friction, or the normal force, not a new force type.

nodes.label: Centrifugal force (fictitious)
nodes.summary: Centrifugal force is a fictitious outward effect seen in a rotating frame, not a real interaction force.

nodes.label: Banked curves and vertical circles
nodes.summary: Banked turns use the normal-force component for centripetal force, and vertical circles vary tension/normal with position.

nodes.label: Period, frequency, and angular speed
nodes.summary: Circular motion is characterized by period, frequency, tangential speed, and angular velocity.

nodes.label: Period and frequency (T = 1/f)
nodes.summary: Period T is the time per revolution and frequency f = 1/T is revolutions per second.

nodes.label: Tangential speed (v = 2πr/T)
nodes.summary: Speed around the circle is v = 2πr/T, the circumference divided by the period.

nodes.label: Angular velocity (ω = 2πf, v = ωr)
nodes.summary: Angular velocity ω = 2πf = 2π/T relates to tangential speed by v = ωr.

nodes.label: Equilibrium, torque, and statics
nodes.summary: Equilibrium requires zero net force and zero net torque, the basis of biomechanical statics (levers).

nodes.label: Translational equilibrium
nodes.summary: Translational equilibrium means the net force on a body is zero.

nodes.label: First condition (ΣF = 0)
nodes.summary: For translational equilibrium the vector sum of all forces equals zero (ΣFx = 0 and ΣFy = 0).

nodes.label: Static vs dynamic equilibrium
nodes.summary: Static equilibrium is rest with zero net force; dynamic equilibrium is constant velocity with zero net force.

nodes.label: Equilibrant force
nodes.summary: The equilibrant is the single force equal and opposite to the resultant that puts a body in equilibrium.

nodes.label: Torque (moment of force)
nodes.summary: Torque is the rotational effect of a force about a pivot.

nodes.label: Torque (τ = rF sinθ)
nodes.summary: Torque τ = rF sinθ measures the rotational effect of a force applied at distance r from the pivot.

nodes.label: Lever arm (moment arm)
nodes.summary: The lever arm is the perpendicular distance from the pivot to the line of action of the force.

nodes.label: Units of torque (N·m)
nodes.summary: Torque is measured in newton-meters (N·m), dimensionally like energy but a distinct vector quantity.

nodes.label: Torque direction (CW vs CCW)
nodes.summary: Torque has a rotational sense, conventionally counterclockwise positive and clockwise negative.

nodes.label: Force couple
nodes.summary: A couple is two equal, opposite, non-collinear forces that produce net torque but zero net force.

nodes.label: Rotational equilibrium
nodes.summary: Rotational equilibrium requires the net torque about any axis to be zero.

nodes.label: Second condition (Στ = 0)
nodes.summary: For rotational equilibrium the sum of clockwise torques equals the sum of counterclockwise torques.

nodes.label: Complete equilibrium (ΣF = 0 and Στ = 0)
nodes.summary: A rigid body is in full equilibrium only when both net force and net torque are zero.

nodes.label: Choosing a pivot to simplify torque analysis
nodes.summary: Because Στ = 0 about any axis, choosing the pivot at an unknown force eliminates it from the torque equation.

nodes.label: Stable, unstable, and neutral equilibrium
nodes.summary: Equilibrium is stable, unstable, or neutral depending on whether a small displacement raises, lowers, or leaves the center of gravity unchanged.

nodes.label: Levers and biomechanics
nodes.summary: The body's joints act as levers classified by the arrangement of fulcrum, load, and effort.

nodes.label: First-class lever
nodes.summary: In a first-class lever the fulcrum lies between the effort and the load (e.g., the atlanto-occipital joint of the head).

nodes.label: Second-class lever
nodes.summary: In a second-class lever the load lies between the fulcrum and effort (e.g., standing on the balls of the feet).

nodes.label: Third-class lever
nodes.summary: In a third-class lever the effort lies between fulcrum and load, common in the body (e.g., biceps flexing the forearm).

nodes.label: Mechanical advantage
nodes.summary: Mechanical advantage is the ratio of load to effort (or effort arm to load arm) a machine provides.

nodes.label: Work, energy, and power
nodes.summary: Work transfers energy, energy is the capacity to do work, and power is the rate of doing work.

nodes.label: Work
nodes.summary: Work is force acting through a displacement, a scalar energy transfer.

nodes.label: Work (W = Fd cosθ)
nodes.summary: Work W = Fd cosθ is done when a force has a component along the displacement.

nodes.label: Positive, negative, and zero work
nodes.summary: Work is positive when force aids motion, negative when it opposes, and zero when perpendicular to displacement.

nodes.label: Joule (unit of work/energy)
nodes.summary: Work and energy are measured in joules, where 1 J = 1 N·m.

nodes.label: Work as area under F–x graph
nodes.summary: For variable force, work equals the area under the force–displacement curve.

nodes.label: Path independence of conservative-force work
nodes.summary: Work by a conservative force (e.g. gravity) depends only on endpoints, not the path taken.

nodes.label: Work by/on a gas (W = PΔV)
nodes.summary: In a constant-pressure process a gas does work W = PΔV against its surroundings.

nodes.label: Kinetic energy
nodes.summary: Kinetic energy is the energy of motion.

nodes.label: Kinetic energy (KE = ½mv²)
nodes.summary: Kinetic energy KE = ½mv² is the energy a mass has due to its motion.

nodes.label: Work–energy theorem (Wnet = ΔKE)
nodes.summary: The net work done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy.

nodes.label: Potential energy
nodes.summary: Potential energy is stored energy of position or configuration.

nodes.label: Gravitational PE (PE = mgh)
nodes.summary: Gravitational potential energy PE = mgh depends on mass, gravity, and height above a reference.

nodes.label: Elastic PE (PE = ½kx²)
nodes.summary: A stretched/compressed spring stores elastic potential energy PE = ½kx².

nodes.label: Reference point dependence of PE
nodes.summary: Potential energy is defined relative to a chosen reference level, so only differences are physically meaningful.

nodes.label: Force as the negative gradient of PE
nodes.summary: A conservative force points toward lower potential energy (F = −dU/dx), the basis of PE-curve analysis.

nodes.label: Conservation of mechanical energy
nodes.summary: Total mechanical energy is conserved when only conservative forces act.

nodes.label: Total mechanical energy (E = KE + PE)
nodes.summary: Total mechanical energy is the sum of kinetic and potential energy.

nodes.label: Conservation of mechanical energy
nodes.summary: With only conservative forces, KE + PE remains constant so KEi + PEi = KEf + PEf.

nodes.label: Conservative vs nonconservative forces
nodes.summary: Conservative forces (gravity, spring) are path-independent; nonconservative forces (friction) dissipate energy.

nodes.label: Energy loss to friction (Wnc = ΔE)
nodes.summary: Nonconservative work equals the change in mechanical energy, e.g. friction converts it to heat.

nodes.label: KE–PE interconversion (pendulum, falling)
nodes.summary: In conservative systems energy continuously trades between kinetic and potential, as in a pendulum or roller coaster.

nodes.label: Conservation of energy (first law of thermodynamics)
nodes.summary: Energy is neither created nor destroyed; it only transforms among forms (ΔU = Q − W).

nodes.label: Power
nodes.summary: Power is the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred.

nodes.label: Power (P = W/t)
nodes.summary: Power P = W/t is the work done per unit time, measured in watts (J/s).

nodes.label: Power as force × velocity (P = Fv)
nodes.summary: Instantaneous power equals force times velocity along the direction of motion (P = Fv cosθ).

nodes.label: Watt and horsepower (units of power)
nodes.summary: Power is measured in watts (1 W = 1 J/s); horsepower is an alternative unit (1 hp ≈ 746 W).

nodes.label: Mechanical efficiency
nodes.summary: Efficiency compares useful output energy to total input energy.

nodes.label: Efficiency (Wout/Win)
nodes.summary: Efficiency is the ratio of useful work or energy output to total energy input, always less than 100%.

nodes.label: Simple machines
nodes.summary: Simple machines trade force for distance to provide mechanical advantage.

nodes.label: Types of simple machines
nodes.summary: Simple machines include the lever, inclined plane, pulley, wedge, screw, and wheel-and-axle.

nodes.label: Inclined plane (force–distance trade-off)
nodes.summary: An inclined plane reduces the force needed to raise a load by increasing the distance over which it acts.

nodes.label: Pulleys and mechanical advantage
nodes.summary: A pulley system can redirect force and multiply it by the number of supporting rope segments.

nodes.label: Work conservation in ideal machines
nodes.summary: An ideal machine conserves work: gains in force are paid for by equal increases in distance (no free energy).

nodes.label: Momentum, impulse, and collisions
nodes.summary: Momentum is conserved in isolated systems, and impulse changes momentum during collisions.

nodes.label: Linear momentum and impulse
nodes.summary: Momentum is mass times velocity, and impulse is the force–time product that changes it.

nodes.label: Momentum (p = mv)
nodes.summary: Linear momentum p = mv is a vector pointing in the direction of velocity.

nodes.label: Impulse (J = FΔt = Δp)
nodes.summary: Impulse J = FΔt equals the change in momentum Δp of an object.

nodes.label: Impulse–momentum theorem
nodes.summary: The impulse delivered to an object equals its change in momentum (FΔt = mΔv).

nodes.label: Impulse as area under F–t graph
nodes.summary: For a varying force, impulse equals the area under the force–time curve.

nodes.label: Extending contact time to reduce force
nodes.summary: For a fixed Δp, increasing collision time decreases the force (basis of airbags and follow-through).

nodes.label: Conservation of momentum
nodes.summary: Total momentum of an isolated system is conserved through interactions.

nodes.label: Conservation of linear momentum
nodes.summary: With no net external force, total system momentum before equals total momentum after (Σpi = Σpf).

nodes.label: Isolated system condition
nodes.summary: Momentum conservation holds only when external forces are negligible during the interaction.

nodes.label: Recoil and explosions
nodes.summary: From rest, conservation of momentum makes fragments recoil with equal and opposite momenta (e.g. gun recoil).

nodes.label: Collisions
nodes.summary: Collisions are classified by whether kinetic energy is conserved.

nodes.label: Elastic collisions
nodes.summary: In an elastic collision both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved.

nodes.label: Inelastic collisions
nodes.summary: In an inelastic collision momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not.

nodes.label: Perfectly inelastic collisions
nodes.summary: In a perfectly inelastic collision the objects stick together and move with a common final velocity.

nodes.label: Kinetic energy lost in inelastic collisions
nodes.summary: Inelastic collisions convert some kinetic energy to heat, sound, or deformation; the most is lost when objects stick.

nodes.label: One- vs two-dimensional collisions
nodes.summary: In two dimensions momentum is conserved independently along each perpendicular axis.

nodes.label: Inclined planes and connected systems
nodes.summary: Applied dynamics problems combine forces on inclines, pulleys, and connected masses.

nodes.label: Forces on an inclined plane
nodes.summary: Gravity on an incline resolves into components parallel and perpendicular to the surface.

nodes.label: Gravity components (mg sinθ, mg cosθ)
nodes.summary: On an incline the parallel weight component is mg sinθ and the perpendicular component is mg cosθ.

nodes.label: Normal force on an incline (N = mg cosθ)
nodes.summary: On a frictionless incline the normal force equals mg cosθ, less than the full weight.

nodes.label: Acceleration down a frictionless incline (a = g sinθ)
nodes.summary: A block slides down a frictionless incline with acceleration a = g sinθ, independent of mass.

nodes.label: Incline with friction (a = g(sinθ − μcosθ))
nodes.summary: With kinetic friction the down-incline acceleration is a = g(sinθ − μk cosθ).

nodes.label: Connected masses and tension
nodes.summary: Masses linked by ropes share a common acceleration analyzed with Newton's second law.

nodes.label: Common acceleration of linked masses
nodes.summary: Connected masses move with one acceleration found by applying ΣF = ma to the whole system.

nodes.label: Atwood machine
nodes.summary: An Atwood machine has two masses over a pulley with a = g(m₁−m₂)/(m₁+m₂).

nodes.label: Tension as an internal force
nodes.summary: Rope tension is found by isolating one mass after solving the whole system for acceleration.

nodes.label: Mechanics in living systems
nodes.summary: Translational mechanics applies to locomotion, muscle/skeletal levers, and bodily forces.

nodes.label: Locomotion and ground forces
nodes.summary: Walking and running rely on friction and Newton's third law against the ground.

nodes.label: Ground reaction force
nodes.summary: The ground pushes back on a foot (Newton's third law), propelling locomotion forward.

nodes.label: Friction for traction
nodes.summary: Static friction between foot and ground provides the traction needed to walk or run without slipping.

nodes.label: Impulse in landing and impact reduction
nodes.summary: Bending the knees on landing lengthens stopping time, lowering the force the body experiences for a given Δp.

nodes.label: Musculoskeletal forces
nodes.summary: Muscles, tendons, and joints generate and transmit forces as biological levers.

nodes.label: Muscle force as tension
nodes.summary: Skeletal muscle exerts tension transmitted through tendons to move bones about joints.

nodes.label: Joint torque and balance
nodes.summary: Muscle force times its lever arm produces the torque that balances loads and moves limbs.

nodes.label: Body levers trade force for speed/range
nodes.summary: Third-class body levers favor speed and range of motion at the cost of requiring large muscle forces (MA < 1).

nodes.label: Stability and center of gravity in the body
nodes.summary: Standing balance keeps the body's center of gravity over the base of support to avoid tipping torque.

external_prereqs.concept_hint: Pressure and PV relationships of gases (fluids/gas phase)
First law of thermodynamics / internal energy and heat (ΔU = Q − W)
Skeletal muscle structure and contraction generating force
Skeletal system: joints and bone as lever framework
Trigonometric relationships used in resolving vectors
Radian measure and angular quantities for rotational kinematics
Fluid viscosity and drag in fluids (Stokes/turbulent drag)
